sections.header: Verses 1’24 — “The LORD Is My Rock” — Cosmic Rescue

heb.paragraph: "I love you, LORD, my strength. The LORD is my rock (salʿî), my fortress and my deliverer" (vv.1–2). Six metaphors for God in two verses: rock, fortress, deliverer, God, refuge, shield, horn of salvation, stronghold. The stack is overwhelming: God is everything David needs.

"The earth trembled and quaked" (v.7). God’s rescue is described as theophany: earthquake, smoke from nostrils, fire from mouth, dark clouds, cherubim-riding, thunder, hail, lightning. This is the most dramatic divine-intervention description in the Psalter.

refs.note: The parallel text — David’s victory song with minor variations.
The Song of the Sea — the exodus rescue that Psalm 18’s theophany echoes.
Habakkuk’s theophany echoes Psalm 18’s cosmic imagery.

notes.note: "I love you, LORD, my strength. The LORD is my rock, my fortress, my deliverer." Six metaphors stacked. Overwhelming protection.
The theophany: earthquake, smoke, fire, darkness, cherubim, thunder, hail, lightning. God enters the battle personally. Creation itself becomes his weapon.
"He reached down from on high and took hold of me; he drew me out of deep waters. He brought me out into a spacious place." From drowning to wide-open space. Rescue is spatial: narrow → broad.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the theophany is the psalm’s literary peak. Nature participates in God’s anger: mountains shake, skies split, fire and hail descend. This is not metaphor but mythic-poetic truth — creation responds to its Creator’s emotion.
Calvin: "he drew me out of deep waters" — Calvin: the waters of death, the waters of despair, the waters of impossible odds. God’s arm reaches into every depth.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: salʿî (my rock/crag) is different from ṣūr (rock). Salʿî is a cliff or crag — high, inaccessible, defensive. God is the elevation that enemies cannot climb.
The NET: "he rode on a cherub and flew" — the cherubim are God’s throne-bearers (Ezek 1, 10). God rides his own throne into battle.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: the six metaphors reflect different aspects of need: rock (stability), fortress (protection), deliverer (rescue), shield (defence), horn (strength), stronghold (safety). David needed every one.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the theophany echoes Sinai (Exod 19). God who came down on the mountain to give the law now comes down to rescue his servant. Same God, same power, personal application.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: "deep waters" is chaos-language (Gen 1:2). God rescues from primordial chaos, not just from human enemies. The rescue is cosmic.

hist.context: The longest psalm so far (50 verses, duplicated in 2 Samuel 22). David praises God for rescue from all enemies. The theophany (vv.7–19) is cosmic: the earth trembles, mountains quake, God rides on a cherub, darkness surrounds him, hailstones and lightning blast the enemy. God reaches down, draws David from deep waters (v.16), sets him in a wide place (v.19). The psalm is retrospective: David reviews his entire career of divine rescue.

sections.header: Verses 25–50 — “You Light My Lamp” — David’s Testimony

heb.paragraph: "You, LORD, light my lamp (nērî); my God turns my darkness into light" (v.28). The lamp is life, hope, guidance. God illuminates what enemies tried to extinguish. The lamp that the wicked’s death snuffs (Prov 13:9), God reignites for the righteous.

refs.note: "Your throne will be established forever" — the Davidic covenant Psalm 18:50 echoes.
Paul quotes Psalm 18:49 for Gentile praise: "I will praise you among the nations."

notes.note: "You light my lamp; my God turns my darkness into light. With your help I can advance against a troop; with my God I can scale a wall." God provides light, strength, and ability beyond David’s natural capacity.

"The LORD lives! Praise be to my Rock! He gives his king great victories; he shows unfailing love to his anointed, to David and his descendants forever." The psalm’s finale: from personal rescue to dynastic promise. The ḥesed to David’s line is forever.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the final verse shifts from first person to third: "his anointed... David and his descendants." The personal becomes dynastic, the testimony becomes prophecy.
Calvin: "to David and his descendants forever" is the Davidic covenant in psalm form. Calvin: every promise to David’s line is ultimately a promise to Christ’s kingdom.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: nēr (lamp) symbolises life and dynasty in the OT (2 Sam 21:17; 1 Kgs 11:36). God keeps the lamp burning when enemies try to extinguish it.
The NET: "his anointed" (məshîḥō) is the same word as Psalm 2:2. The messianic thread connects the Psalter’s introduction to its first great victory psalm.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: "to David and his descendants forever" extends the psalm from personal testimony to messianic promise. Christ is the ultimate descendant who receives eternal ḥesed.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the second half is David’s testimony — retrospective gratitude for a lifetime of divine faithfulness. The messianic close (v.50) ensures the testimony outlasts David.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: "you light my lamp" — in a world without electricity, a lamp is survival. God provides what darkness cannot take: visibility, warmth, hope.

hist.context: David reflects on God’s faithfulness: to the faithful God is faithful, to the pure he is pure (vv.25–26). God lights his lamp, enables him to leap walls, provides a perfect way (vv.28–30). God arms him for battle, gives him a wide path so his feet don’t slip (vv.32–36). David pursues enemies, shatters them, stamps them like dust (vv.37–42). God made him head of nations (v.43). The psalm closes with a messianic declaration: "He gives his king great victories; he shows unfailing love to his anointed, to David and his descendants forever" (v.50).

ppl.text: David praises God for rescue from all enemies…

ppl.text: David praises God for rescue from all enemies…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThe cords of the grave coiled around me; the snares of death confronted me.ESVthe cords of Sheol entangled me; the snares of death confronted me.NIVSmoke rose from his nostrils; consuming fire came from his mouth, burning coals blazed out of it.ESVSmoke went up from his nostrils, and devouring fire from his mouth; glowing coals flamed forth from him.

rec.text: ""I love you, LORD, my strength. The LORD is my rock, my fortress, my deliverer." Six metaphors stacked. Overwhelming protection."

rows.label: The Testimony — 18:25–50
rows.text: "You light my lamp." Battle strength. "Ḥesed to David and his descendants forever."

hebtext.note: "I love you, LORD, my strength. The LORD is my rock (salʿî), my fortress and my deliverer" (vv.1–2). Six metaphors for God in two verses: rock, fortress, deliverer, God, refuge, shield, horn of salvation, stronghold. The stack is overwhelming: God is everything David needs.

"The earth trembled and quaked" (v.7). God’s rescue is described as theophany: earthquake, smoke from nostrils, fire from mouth, dark clouds, cherubim-riding, thunder, hail, lightning. This is the most dramatic divine-intervention description in the Psalter.

"You, LORD, light my lamp (nērî); my God turns my darkness into light" (v.28). The lamp is life, hope, guidance. God illuminates what enemies tried to extinguish. The lamp that the wicked’s death snuffs (Prov 13:9), God reignites for the righteous.

thread.text: The parallel text — David’s victory song with minor variations.
The Song of the Sea — the exodus rescue that Psalm 18’s theophany echoes.
Habakkuk’s theophany echoes Psalm 18’s cosmic imagery.
"Your throne will be established forever" — the Davidic covenant Psalm 18:50 echoes.
Paul quotes Psalm 18:49 for Gentile praise: "I will praise you among the nations."

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks: the theophany is God’s most dramatic intervention in the Psalter. Prophecy: Davidic covenant and messianic promise. Covenant: ḥesed to David’s line forever.
